overview: Oral anticoagulant requiring mandatory INR monitoring. Inhibits Vitamin K-dependent coagulation factors (II, VII, IX, X) and anticoagulant proteins C and S. Multiple food and drug interactions make management complex. Has been the standard oral anticoagulant for 60+ years — now largely replaced by DOACs for many indications but still required for mechanical heart valves and where DOACs are unsuitable.
dosage: Individualised: target INR 2–3 (most indications); 2.5–3.5 (mechanical mitral valve). Usual starting dose 5–10 mg OD (3 mg in elderly, liver disease, heart failure). Adjust based on INR.
mechanism: Inhibits Vitamin K epoxide reductase complex (VKORC1) → prevents regeneration of reduced Vitamin K → ↓ gamma-carboxylation of factors II, VII, IX, X, and proteins C and S.
primary_indications: AF — stroke prevention (non-valvular and valvular), DVT/PE treatment and secondary prevention, Mechanical prosthetic heart valves (DOACs contraindicated), Antiphospholipid syndrome (high-titre — DOACs less effective), Cardiomyopathy with mural thrombus
side_effects: Bleeding (major risk — any site; intracranial most serious); Warfarin skin necrosis (rare — early, protein C deficiency — protein C has shorter half-life); Purple toe syndrome (rare — cholesterol emboli); Teratogenic (warfarin embryopathy — 6–12 weeks gestation); Alopecia
nursing_considerations: INR MONITORING: frequent initially (daily until stable → weekly → monthly when stable); critical to give result and dose adjustment at every contact; HUGE DRUG INTERACTIONS: antibiotics, NSAIDs, antifungals, amiodarone (doubles warfarin effect), rifampicin (halves effect), alcohol, herbal remedies — check with every new prescription or change; VITAMIN K: consistent diet — do NOT avoid leafy greens entirely; eat CONSISTENT amounts (broccoli, spinach, kale — not varying week to week); WARFARIN ALERT CARD: patient must carry and show to all healthcare providers; OVERDOSE/BLEEDING: reversal options — Vitamin K (slow, 6–12 hrs), FFP (immediate), prothrombin complex concentrate (PCC — fastest, gold standard); PREGNANCY: absolutely contraindicated (weeks 6–12 embryopathy; throughout — fetal haemorrhage); switch to LMWH; Target INR range must be clearly documented for each patient — mechanical valves have higher targets
contraindications: Active bleeding, Pregnancy (especially 6–12 weeks), Severe hypertension (haemorrhagic stroke risk), Recent neurosurgery or ophthalmic surgery, Haemorrhagic stroke, Alcoholism (variable diet and liver disease)

overview: Most widely used LMWH. Predictable pharmacokinetics → fixed weight-based dosing without routine monitoring (except in extremes of weight, renal impairment, pregnancy). Less HIT than UFH. SC injection only (not IV bolus for therapeutic dosing). Anti-Xa monitoring in specific populations.
dosage: DVT/PE treatment: 1 mg/kg SC BD or 1.5 mg/kg SC OD. Prophylaxis: 40 mg SC OD (20 mg if eGFR <20). ACS: 1 mg/kg SC BD.
mechanism: Binds ATIII → preferential anti-Xa activity (anti-Xa:anti-IIa ratio 3.8:1 vs 1:1 for UFH). Predictable pharmacokinetics — no plasma protein binding variability.
primary_indications: DVT/PE — treatment and secondary prevention, DVT prophylaxis (surgical and medical), ACS (NSTEMI, STEMI adjunct — ESSENCE, SYNERGY trials), Bridge anticoagulation, Antiphospholipid syndrome, Cancer-associated thrombosis
side_effects: Bleeding; HIT (less common than UFH — less anti-IIa activity); Injection site bruising and haematoma; Thrombocytopaenia; Hyperkalaemia; Osteoporosis (long-term); Hypersensitivity
nursing_considerations: SC INJECTION TECHNIQUE: abdomen (alternating sides, 5 cm from navel), anterolateral thigh, or lateral arm; 90° angle; do NOT expel air bubble from pre-loaded syringe (included to clear needle after injection); Do NOT aspirate before injection (bruising); RENAL IMPAIRMENT: dose reduction required; anti-Xa monitoring if eGFR <30 (use UFH in severe renal failure); ANTI-Xa MONITORING required in: obesity (>100 kg), low body weight (<50 kg), renal impairment, pregnancy — target 0.5–1.0 IU/mL BD dosing (4 hrs post-injection); REVERSAL: protamine sulphate partially reverses (neutralises anti-IIa and ~50–60% anti-Xa); 1 mg protamine per 1 mg enoxaparin; HIT: less common but possible — monitor platelets as per UFH protocol if high risk
contraindications: History of HIT, Active major bleeding, Severe thrombocytopaenia, Prosthetic heart valve (insufficient evidence — use UFH)

overview: IV anticoagulant with rapid onset; monitored by APTT; reversible with protamine.
dosage: IV bolus: 5000 units, then infusion 15–25 units/kg/hr. Titrate to APTT 60–100 seconds.
mechanism: Binds antithrombin III → inhibits thrombin and Factor Xa.
primary_indications: DVT/PE (initial treatment), ACS, Cardiopulmonary bypass, Renal failure (preferred over LMWH), Heparin infusions in ICU
side_effects: Bleeding; HIT (5% — serious); Hyperkalaemia; Osteoporosis (long-term); Thrombocytopaenia
nursing_considerations: Monitor APTT 6-hourly and adjust infusion; Monitor platelets for HIT (days 5–10); If platelet drop >50% — suspect HIT, stop heparin immediately; Reversal: protamine sulphate; Different concentrations exist — always check label
contraindications: Active bleeding, HIT history, Severe thrombocytopaenia

overview: First-line oral antidiabetic; reduces hepatic glucose production; lactic acidosis risk.
dosage: 500 mg PO BD with meals; titrate to 1 g BD–TDS. Max 3 g/day.
mechanism: Activates AMPK → ↓ hepatic gluconeogenesis and glycogenolysis; ↑ peripheral glucose uptake.
primary_indications: Type 2 diabetes mellitus (1st line), Prediabetes, Polycystic ovary syndrome (PCOS — off-label)
side_effects: GI: nausea, diarrhoea, abdominal pain (transient); Lactic acidosis (rare, serious); Vitamin B12 deficiency (long-term); Metallic taste
nursing_considerations: Take with meals to reduce GI effects; HOLD 48 hrs before/after IV contrast (lactic acidosis); HOLD if acute illness, surgery, or dehydration; Monitor B12 annually; Hold if eGFR <30; Does NOT cause hypoglycaemia as monotherapy
contraindications: eGFR <30 mL/min, Hepatic failure, Lactic acidosis history, Alcohol excess, IV contrast (hold 48 hrs)

overview: Sulphonylurea that stimulates insulin release; significant hypoglycaemia risk; long duration.
dosage: 2.5–5 mg PO OD–BD with meals. Max 20 mg/day.
mechanism: Blocks ATP-sensitive K⁺ channels in pancreatic β-cells → depolarisation → insulin secretion.
primary_indications: Type 2 diabetes mellitus
side_effects: Hypoglycaemia (major, prolonged); Weight gain; GI upset; Haemolytic anaemia (G6PD); Photosensitivity
nursing_considerations: Hypoglycaemia is the main risk — educate patient to recognise symptoms; Give with meals; Avoid in elderly (prolonged severe hypoglycaemia); Avoid in renal failure; Check G6PD status
contraindications: Type 1 diabetes, Ketoacidosis, Renal/hepatic impairment, Pregnancy

overview: Short-acting soluble insulin; used for mealtime glucose control and DKA treatment.
dosage: SC: 4–10 units 30 min before meals. DKA infusion: 0.1 units/kg/hr IV. Individualised.
mechanism: Binds insulin receptors → ↑ glucose uptake into cells, ↓ gluconeogenesis, ↑ glycogen synthesis.
primary_indications: Type 1 and 2 diabetes, Diabetic ketoacidosis (DKA), Hyperglycaemic hyperosmolar state (HHS), Hyperkalaemia (with dextrose), Peri-operative glucose management
side_effects: Hypoglycaemia (major); Hypokalaemia (shifts K⁺ intracellularly); Weight gain; Lipodystrophy at injection site; Oedema (initial)
nursing_considerations: TWO nurse check mandatory for insulin preparation and administration; NEVER abbreviate ;  as ;  — causes 10× overdose errors; Rotate injection sites; Monitor blood glucose before and after; Treat hypo with 15 g fast-acting carbs (15-15 rule); SC: give 30 min before meal
contraindications: Hypoglycaemia (active)

overview: Once-daily basal insulin analogue; provides steady 24-hr coverage without peaks.
dosage: 10 units SC OD at same time each day; titrate based on fasting glucose. Individualised.
mechanism: Slow absorption from SC depot → steady, peakless insulin release over 24 hrs.
primary_indications: Type 1 and 2 diabetes (basal insulin)
side_effects: Hypoglycaemia (less than NPH insulin); Weight gain; Lipodystrophy; Injection site reactions
nursing_considerations: Inject at same time every day; Cannot be mixed with other insulins; Use a new injection site each time; Clear solution — discard if cloudy or discoloured; Store unopened pens in fridge; open pen at room temp
contraindications: Hypoglycaemia (active)

overview: SGLT2 inhibitor with proven cardiovascular and renal mortality benefit beyond glucose lowering.
dosage: 10 mg PO OD; may increase to 25 mg OD.
mechanism: Inhibits SGLT2 in proximal tubule → ↓ glucose reabsorption → glycosuria.
primary_indications: Type 2 diabetes, Heart failure with reduced ejection fraction (HFrEF), Chronic kidney disease
side_effects: Genital mycotic infections (thrush); UTI; DKA (euglycaemic — normal glucose); Polyuria; Fournier gangrene (rare); Volume depletion
nursing_considerations: Genital hygiene education to prevent thrush; Hold 3 days before surgery (DKA risk); HOLD if fasting/sick day rules; Urinalysis if DKA suspected even with normal glucose; Volume depletion — monitor BP and hydration; Benefit in heart failure independent of diabetes
contraindications: eGFR <20 (type 2 DM indication), Type 1 diabetes (DKA risk), Recurrent UTIs

overview: Oral DPP-4 inhibitor that increases GLP-1 and GIP levels; weight-neutral.
dosage: 100 mg PO OD. Renal impairment: 50 mg (eGFR 30–50) or 25 mg (eGFR <30).
mechanism: Inhibits DPP-4 enzyme → ↑ active GLP-1 and GIP → ↑ glucose-dependent insulin secretion, ↓ glucagon.
primary_indications: Type 2 diabetes mellitus (monotherapy or combination)
side_effects: Nasopharyngitis; Pancreatitis (rare); Joint pain (arthralgia); Upper respiratory tract infections
nursing_considerations: Low hypoglycaemia risk as monotherapy; Renal dose adjustment; Monitor for signs of pancreatitis; Weight neutral — no gain or loss
contraindications: Type 1 diabetes, Pancreatitis history, Severe renal impairment (adjust dose)

overview: First-line bronchodilator for acute asthma and COPD exacerbation.
dosage: Inhaler: 100–200 mcg (1–2 puffs) PRN up to 4× daily. Severe asthma: 2.5–5 mg nebulised every 20 min. IV: 250 mcg slow bolus.
mechanism: Selective β2-adrenoceptor agonism → bronchial smooth muscle relaxation → bronchodilation.
primary_indications: Acute asthma (first-line relief), COPD exacerbation, Bronchospasm, Hyperkalaemia (nebulised — adjunct), Exercise-induced bronchoconstriction
side_effects: Tachycardia; Tremor (fine, peripheral); Hypokalaemia (high doses); Headache; Hypomagnesaemia (nebulised)
nursing_considerations: Spacer device improves MDI delivery; Inhaler technique is critical; Overuse (>3 canisters/month) indicates poor asthma control; Monitor K⁺ with high-dose nebulised therapy; Shake MDI before each use
contraindications: Hypersensitivity to salbutamol

overview: Inhaled corticosteroid; mainstay of asthma maintenance therapy.
dosage: Low dose: 100–200 mcg/day. Moderate: 200–500 mcg/day. High: >500 mcg/day. Divided BD.
mechanism: Glucocorticoid receptor activation → ↓ inflammatory mediators → ↓ airway inflammation.
primary_indications: Asthma maintenance (step 2 and above), COPD (with recurrent exacerbations)
side_effects: Oral candidiasis (thrush); Dysphonia (hoarseness); Adrenal suppression (high doses); Osteoporosis (long-term high dose); Growth retardation in children
nursing_considerations: Rinse mouth and gargle after every dose (prevent candidiasis); Use spacer to reduce oropharyngeal deposition; Not for acute asthma attacks; Do not stop abruptly if on high doses; Regular bone density checks if on high doses
contraindications: Acute asthma/status asthmaticus (use bronchodilator first)

overview: Once-daily inhaled anticholinergic; first-line maintenance bronchodilator for COPD.
dosage: 18 mcg inhaled OD (Handihaler) or 5 mcg OD (Respimat).
mechanism: Muscarinic M3 receptor blockade → bronchial smooth muscle relaxation → bronchodilation.
primary_indications: COPD maintenance, Severe asthma (add-on)
side_effects: Dry mouth; Urinary retention; Constipation; Blurred vision; Tachycardia
nursing_considerations: Use only HandiHaler or Respimat device — not MDI; Capsules for inhalation — NOT to be swallowed; Teach correct device technique; Caution in BPH and narrow-angle glaucoma; Once daily = adherence advantage
contraindications: Narrow-angle glaucoma, Urinary retention/BPH, Hypersensitivity

overview: Oral add-on asthma therapy; also effective for allergic rhinitis.
dosage: Adults: 10 mg PO OD at night. Children 6–14: 5 mg.
mechanism: Blocks cysteinyl leukotriene receptors → ↓ bronchoconstriction and inflammation.
primary_indications: Asthma (adjunct, especially aspirin-sensitive), Allergic rhinitis, Exercise-induced bronchoconstriction
side_effects: Headache; Neuropsychiatric effects (mood changes, depression, suicidal ideation); GI upset; Elevated liver enzymes
nursing_considerations: FDA warning: neuropsychiatric adverse effects — screen mood at each visit; Take at bedtime; Not for acute attacks; Particularly useful in aspirin-exacerbated asthma
contraindications: Hypersensitivity, Active psychiatric illness (relative)

overview: Oral corticosteroid for acute exacerbations of asthma, COPD, and inflammatory conditions.
dosage: Asthma exacerbation: 40–50 mg OD × 5 days. Maintenance (rheumatological): 5–15 mg OD. Replacement: 5–7.5 mg OD.
mechanism: Binds glucocorticoid receptors → ↓ inflammatory cytokines → broad anti-inflammatory and immunosuppressive effects.
primary_indications: Asthma exacerbation, COPD exacerbation, Rheumatoid arthritis, IBD, Nephrotic syndrome, Immunosuppression post-transplant, Addison disease replacement
side_effects: HPA axis suppression; Hyperglycaemia; Osteoporosis; Cushing syndrome (long-term); Peptic ulcer; Weight gain; Hypertension; Increased infection risk; Mood changes/psychosis; Adrenal crisis on abrupt withdrawal
nursing_considerations: NEVER stop abruptly if >3 weeks — taper (adrenal crisis risk); Take with food (GI protection); Steroid card for patients on long-term steroids; Monitor blood glucose; Bone protection: calcium + Vitamin D + bisphosphonate if long-term; Increased infection susceptibility — avoid sick contacts
contraindications: Systemic infection (unless life-saving), Live vaccines during immunosuppression

overview: Broad-spectrum oral penicillin; first-line for community respiratory, ENT, and urinary infections.
dosage: 250–500 mg PO TDS or 875 mg BD. Severe: 1 g TDS. Children: 25 mg/kg/day in 3 doses.
mechanism: Inhibits bacterial cell wall synthesis (PBP binding) → cell lysis. Susceptible to beta-lactamases.
primary_indications: Community-acquired pneumonia, Otitis media, Sinusitis, Strep throat, UTI, H. pylori (triple therapy)
side_effects: Diarrhoea; Rash (especially in EBV mononucleosis); Nausea; Allergic reaction (urticaria, anaphylaxis); Clostridium difficile (disrupts gut flora)
nursing_considerations: Ask about penicillin allergy before giving; If infectious mononucleosis suspected — avoid (causes widespread maculopapular rash); Complete full course; Monitor for anaphylaxis (especially first dose); Can be given with or without food
contraindications: Penicillin allergy, Infectious mononucleosis

overview: Amoxicillin + clavulanic acid; beta-lactamase inhibitor extends spectrum to resistant organisms.
dosage: 625 mg (500/125) PO TDS or 1.2 g IV TDS.
mechanism: Amoxicillin inhibits cell wall synthesis; clavulanate irreversibly inhibits beta-lactamase enzymes.
primary_indications: Lower respiratory tract infection, Skin and soft tissue infection, Dental infections, UTI, Bite wounds, Surgical prophylaxis
side_effects: GI disturbance (high incidence); Cholestatic hepatitis (especially with clavulanate); Rash; Allergic reaction
nursing_considerations: Take with food — reduces GI side effects; LFTs if used >14 days or history of hepatic disease; Cholestatic jaundice can occur weeks after completing treatment; Penicillin allergy check essential
contraindications: Penicillin allergy, History of cholestatic jaundice with co-amoxiclav, Infectious mononucleosis

overview: Broad-spectrum fluoroquinolone with excellent Gram-negative coverage; tendon rupture risk.
dosage: UTI: 250–500 mg BD. Severe infection: 400 mg IV BD or 750 mg PO BD.
mechanism: Inhibits bacterial DNA gyrase and topoisomerase IV → DNA damage → cell death.
primary_indications: UTI (complicated), Respiratory tract infections, GI infections (Salmonella, Shigella, Campylobacter), Anthrax prophylaxis, Bone/joint infections
side_effects: Tendinopathy and tendon rupture (Achilles); QT prolongation; Photosensitivity; CNS effects (insomnia, seizures); Cartilage damage in growing children; Clostridioides difficile; Peripheral neuropathy
nursing_considerations: Avoid in children/adolescents (cartilage toxicity); Tendon pain: STOP immediately (especially if on steroids or elderly); Avoid in pregnancy; Take 2 hrs before or 6 hrs after antacids/iron (chelation); Sunscreen during treatment; QTc monitoring if risk factors
contraindications: Children/adolescents (growth restriction), Pregnancy, History of tendon disorders, QT prolongation

overview: Antianaerobic and antiprotozoal drug; effective against C. difficile and amoebiasis.
dosage: 400–500 mg PO TDS or 500 mg IV TDS. C. diff: 400 mg TDS × 10 days. Giardia: 400 mg TDS × 5 days.
mechanism: Nitroimidazole reduced to toxic metabolites inside anaerobic organisms → DNA strand breaks.
primary_indications: Anaerobic bacterial infections, Clostridium difficile (mild-moderate), Bacterial vaginosis, Trichomoniasis, Amoebiasis, H. pylori (triple therapy), Dental infections, Surgical prophylaxis
side_effects: Metallic taste; Nausea, GI upset; Peripheral neuropathy (prolonged use); Disulfiram-like reaction with alcohol; Peripheral neuropathy
nursing_considerations: STRICT no alcohol during treatment and 48 hrs after (severe reaction: flushing, vomiting); Take with food; Warn about metallic taste; IV version has less GI side effects; Peripheral neuropathy with prolonged use
contraindications: First trimester pregnancy (avoid if possible), Alcohol (absolute), Disulfiram use

overview: Broad-spectrum 3rd generation cephalosporin; once-daily IV/IM for severe infections.
dosage: 1–2 g IV/IM OD. Meningitis: 2–4 g OD. Gonorrhoea: 500 mg IM single dose.
mechanism: Beta-lactam binds PBPs → inhibits cell wall synthesis → bacterial lysis.
primary_indications: Community-acquired pneumonia (severe), Meningitis, Gonorrhoea, Sepsis, Typhoid fever, Pelvic inflammatory disease
side_effects: Diarrhoea; Injection site pain (IM); Allergic reaction; Biliary pseudolithiasis; C. difficile
nursing_considerations: Cross-allergy with penicillins (<2%); Can be given IM (dilute with lidocaine for pain); Do not give calcium-containing IV fluids simultaneously (precipitation); Once daily dosing improves compliance; LFTs monitoring if prolonged use
contraindications: Cephalosporin hypersensitivity, Neonates with jaundice (biliary sludge), Hyperbilirubinaemia in neonates

overview: Glycopeptide antibiotic; last resort for MRSA and oral for C. difficile.
dosage: IV: 15–20 mg/kg every 8–12 hrs (TDM essential). PO (C.diff): 125 mg QDS × 10 days.
mechanism: Inhibits cell wall peptidoglycan synthesis (different site to beta-lactams).
primary_indications: MRSA infections, C. difficile (PO — severe/recurrent), Infective endocarditis (penicillin-allergic), Hospital-acquired pneumonia
side_effects: Red Man Syndrome (if infused too fast — not allergy); Vanc ototoxicity and nephrotoxicity; , ; , ; ,
nursing_considerations: Infuse slowly over ≥60 min (Red Man Syndrome: pruritus and flushing if too fast); TDM: trough level before 4th dose (target 10–20 mg/L); Monitor creatinine and urine output; Oral vancomycin NOT absorbed systemically (stays in gut for C.diff); Dedicated IV line — incompatible with many drugs
contraindications: Hypersensitivity to vancomycin

overview: Bactericidal aminoglycoside for Gram-negative and synergistic endocarditis cover; nephrotoxic/ototoxic.
dosage: 5–7 mg/kg IV OD (once daily dosing). Neonates: individualised. TDM essential.
mechanism: Binds 30S ribosomal subunit → inhibits bacterial protein synthesis → bactericidal.
primary_indications: Severe Gram-negative infections, Endocarditis (synergy with penicillin), Septicaemia, Neonatal sepsis, Pyelonephritis (IV)
side_effects: Nephrotoxicity (dose-dependent, reversible); Ototoxicity (irreversible — vestibular and auditory); Neuromuscular blockade (rare)
nursing_considerations: Serum level monitoring mandatory (trough and peak or Hartmann chart); Monitor creatinine daily; Pre-dose level: <1 mg/L (to prevent accumulation); Monitor for tinnitus, dizziness — irreversible hearing loss; Avoid with other nephrotoxic or ototoxic drugs; Use with extreme caution in renal impairment
contraindications: Myasthenia gravis, Severe renal impairment (adjust dose/frequency), Hypersensitivity to aminoglycosides

overview: Once-daily macrolide antibiotic; excellent tissue penetration and anti-inflammatory properties.
dosage: CAP: 500 mg OD × 3 days or 500 mg day 1 then 250 mg days 2–5. STI: 1 g single dose.
mechanism: Binds 50S ribosomal subunit → inhibits protein synthesis. Bacteriostatic (sometimes bactericidal).
primary_indications: Community-acquired pneumonia, Atypical pneumonia (Mycoplasma, Chlamydia), STIs (chlamydia), Skin and soft tissue infection (penicillin allergy), Pertussis
side_effects: GI upset (nausea, diarrhoea); QT prolongation; Hepatotoxicity; Hearing loss (prolonged use); Ototoxicity
nursing_considerations: QTc monitoring if risk factors; Can be taken with or without food; Excellent for atypical organisms (Mycoplasma); Single-dose (1 g) for chlamydia — confirm partner treatment; Do not use with other QT-prolonging drugs
contraindications: QT prolongation, Macrolide hypersensitivity, Hepatic disease (caution)

overview: Broad-spectrum tetracycline; effective for atypical organisms, STIs, malaria, and acne.
dosage: 100 mg PO BD. Malaria prophylaxis: 100 mg OD (start 2 days before, continue 4 weeks after).
mechanism: Binds 30S ribosomal subunit → inhibits protein synthesis. Bacteriostatic.
primary_indications: Atypical pneumonia, Chlamydia/STIs, Malaria prophylaxis and treatment, Lyme disease, Acne (low dose), Rosacea, Rickettsial disease
side_effects: Photosensitivity (significant); Oesophageal irritation; GI upset; Dental discolouration (children); Hepatotoxicity; Intracranial hypertension (pseudotumour cerebri)
nursing_considerations: Take with full glass of water — sit upright for 30 min (prevent oesophageal ulceration); High SPF sunscreen — avoid sun exposure; Do NOT give to children <8 or pregnant women (dental/bone effects); Antacids and dairy reduce absorption (take 2 hrs apart); Can be taken with food (reduces GI upset)
contraindications: Children <8 years, Pregnancy and breastfeeding, Hypersensitivity to tetracyclines

overview: First-line ACT for uncomplicated P. falciparum malaria; highly effective.
dosage: Adults (≥35 kg): 4 tabs at 0, 8, 24, 36, 48, 60 hrs (6-dose regimen).
mechanism: Artemether: rapid action, kills ring-stage parasites; Lumefantrine: eliminates residual parasites (longer half-life).
primary_indications: Uncomplicated P. falciparum malaria, Mixed malaria infections
side_effects: Headache; Dizziness; Nausea; Abdominal pain; QT prolongation (lumefantrine); Arthralgia
nursing_considerations: MUST be taken with food or milk (fat increases lumefantrine absorption); Completion of all 6 doses is essential; QTc monitoring if cardiac risk factors; Repeat dose if vomiting within 1 hr of dose; Test for G6PD before use in endemic areas
contraindications: 1st trimester pregnancy (use quinine), Hypersensitivity, QT prolongation

overview: IV/IM treatment for severe and complicated malaria; first choice over quinine.
dosage: 2.4 mg/kg IV at 0, 12, and 24 hrs, then daily. Switch to oral ACT when tolerated.
mechanism: Rapidly kills all stages of asexual P. falciparum parasite via free radical generation.
primary_indications: Severe P. falciparum malaria, Complicated malaria (cerebral, renal, severe anaemia)
side_effects: Haemolysis (post-treatment haemolytic anaemia — delayed 2–4 weeks); Neurotoxicity (high doses — animal data); GI effects
nursing_considerations: POST-TREATMENT HAEMOLYSIS: monitor Hb/reticulocytes for 4 weeks; Switch to oral ACT as soon as patient can tolerate PO; First choice over IV quinine for severe malaria; Reconstitute with sodium bicarbonate then dilute with normal saline
contraindications: Hypersensitivity

overview: Classic antimalarial; limited use due to widespread resistance; used in sensitive areas and rheumatology.
dosage: Malaria treatment: 600 mg base stat, 300 mg at 6 hrs, then 300 mg OD × 2 days.
mechanism: Accumulates in parasite food vacuole → inhibits haem polymerisation → toxic haem accumulation.
primary_indications: P. vivax, P. malariae (where susceptible), Rheumatoid arthritis (hydroxychloroquine preferred), Lupus (hydroxychloroquine preferred)
side_effects: GI upset; Pruritus (common in dark-skinned individuals); Retinopathy (long-term); QT prolongation; Neuropsychiatric effects; Cardiomyopathy (chronic)
nursing_considerations: Retinal screening annually if on long-term therapy; G6PD screening; Baseline and periodic ophthalmology review; Pruritus can be severe — antihistamines may help; Resistance widespread — check local patterns
contraindications: P. falciparum (resistance), Retinal disease, G6PD deficiency (relative), Psoriasis

overview: IV quinine for severe malaria when artesunate unavailable; narrow therapeutic index.
dosage: Loading: 20 mg/kg IV over 4 hrs, then 10 mg/kg every 8 hrs.
mechanism: Inhibits haem polymerisation in parasite + inhibits parasite DNA replication.
primary_indications: Severe falciparum malaria (2nd line after artesunate), Uncomplicated malaria in 1st trimester pregnancy (PO)
side_effects: Cinchonism: tinnitus, headache, dizziness, nausea; Hypoglycaemia (stimulates insulin); QT prolongation and arrhythmias; Haemolytic anaemia (G6PD); Thrombocytopaenia
nursing_considerations: Cardiac monitoring (QTc) during IV infusion; Blood glucose monitoring — significant hypoglycaemia risk; MUST be given in dextrose (not saline) to prevent hypoglycaemia; Avoid rapid infusion — cardiac toxicity; Cinchonism symptoms are common — reassure
contraindications: Optic neuritis, Tinnitus, Haemoglobinuria, Myasthenia gravis

overview: Backbone NRTI used in first-line HIV regimens; also active against Hepatitis B.
dosage: 300 mg PO OD with or without food.
mechanism: Prodrug → tenofovir diphosphate → competes with dATP → chain termination of reverse transcriptase.
primary_indications: HIV-1 infection (combination ART), Hepatitis B virus infection, HIV PrEP (with emtricitabine)
side_effects: Renal toxicity (tubular dysfunction, Fanconi syndrome); Decreased bone mineral density; Nausea, GI upset; Lactic acidosis (rare)
nursing_considerations: Monitor creatinine and urinalysis 3-monthly; Bone density monitoring in high-risk patients; Do not use with other nephrotoxic drugs (NSAIDs, aminoglycosides); Adherence counselling critical — HIV resistance with missed doses; Effective against HBV — ;  on discontinuation in HBV co-infection
contraindications: eGFR <30 (adjust dosing)

overview: NNRTI used in first-line ART; CNS side effects common initially.
dosage: 600 mg PO OD at bedtime (reduces CNS side effects).
mechanism: Binds allosteric site on HIV reverse transcriptase → conformational change → inhibits DNA synthesis.
primary_indications: HIV-1 (combination ART — first-line in many guidelines)
side_effects: CNS: vivid dreams, dizziness, insomnia, depression (first 2–4 weeks); Rash (can be severe — SJS rare); Teratogenic (neural tube defects); Elevated liver enzymes; Dyslipidaemia
nursing_considerations: Take at bedtime (CNS effects improve with sleep); Warn about vivid dreams for 2–4 weeks (resolve); CONTRAINDICATED in 1st trimester pregnancy; Screen for depression before and during; Enzyme inducer — many drug interactions; Avoid in psychiatric illness
contraindications: 1st trimester pregnancy, Severe hepatic impairment, Psychiatric illness (relative)

overview: Preferred first-line INSTI; high barrier to resistance, excellent tolerability.
dosage: 50 mg PO OD (treatment-naive). Twice daily if certain drug interactions.
mechanism: Inhibits HIV integrase → prevents viral DNA integration into host cell genome.
primary_indications: HIV-1 infection (combination ART — preferred first-line)
side_effects: Insomnia; Headache; Neural tube defects (periconception); Weight gain; Elevated creatinine (inhibits tubular secretion — not true renal impairment)
nursing_considerations: Preferred over efavirenz (better CNS profile); Neural tube defect risk — counsel women of childbearing age; Antacids: take 2 hrs apart (chelation); Weight gain may be significant; Elevation of creatinine is pharmacological — not actual renal damage
contraindications: 1st trimester pregnancy/periconceptional (neural tube defect risk), Dofetilide co-administration

overview: First antiretroviral drug; now used mainly in PMTCT and as component of ART.
dosage: 300 mg PO BD or 200 mg TDS.
mechanism: Thymidine analogue → incorporated into viral DNA → chain termination.
primary_indications: HIV-1 (combination ART), PMTCT (prevention of mother-to-child transmission), Post-exposure prophylaxis (PEP) — combined
side_effects: Bone marrow suppression (anaemia, neutropaenia); Myopathy; Nausea, headache; Hyperlactatemia; Lipoatrophy
nursing_considerations: Monitor FBC (Hb and neutrophils) monthly; Headache and nausea usually improve after 4 weeks; Lipoatrophy: loss of subcutaneous fat — discuss before starting; PMTCT: give to mother during labour and infant for 6 weeks
contraindications: Severe anaemia (Hb <7 g/dL), Neutropaenia (<500/mm³)

overview: Cornerstone of TB treatment; hepatotoxic; causes peripheral neuropathy — give with pyridoxine.
dosage: Active TB: 5 mg/kg OD (max 300 mg/day). Latent: 300 mg OD × 6 months.
mechanism: Inhibits enoyl-ACP reductase (InhA) → disrupts mycolic acid synthesis → mycobacterial cell wall destruction.
primary_indications: Active tuberculosis (combination with rifampicin, pyrazinamide, ethambutol), Latent TB prophylaxis (6 months), TB meningitis
side_effects: Hepatotoxicity (monitor LFTs); Peripheral neuropathy (B6 deficiency — give pyridoxine); Rash; Drug-induced lupus; Seizures (in overdose); Psychosis (rare)
nursing_considerations: ALWAYS give with Pyridoxine 25 mg/day (prevents neuropathy); LFTs at baseline and monthly (stop if >3× ULN); Acetylator status (slow acetylators get higher levels); Multiple drug interactions (CYP450 inhibitor); Ask about alcohol — increases hepatotoxicity; DOT (Directly Observed Therapy) preferred
contraindications: Active hepatic disease, INH-related hepatotoxicity history

overview: Key first-line TB drug; potent enzyme inducer affecting many medications.
dosage: 10 mg/kg OD (max 600 mg) on empty stomach.
mechanism: Inhibits bacterial DNA-dependent RNA polymerase → ↓ RNA synthesis → bactericidal.
primary_indications: Tuberculosis (first-line), Leprosy, Staphylococcal infections (adjunct), Meningococcal prophylaxis
side_effects: Red-orange discolouration of body fluids (urine, tears, sweat); Hepatotoxicity; Drug interactions (CYP450 inducer — MASSIVE); Flu-like syndrome (intermittent dosing); Thrombocytopaenia
nursing_considerations: WARN: urine, tears, saliva, sweat will turn orange-red (stains soft contact lenses); Take on empty stomach (30 min before food); MAJOR drug interactions: reduces efficacy of OCP, warfarin, ART, steroids, anticonvulsants; LFTs monitoring; Women on OCP: alternative contraception needed; Do not use contact lenses
contraindications: Jaundice, Rifampicin hypersensitivity

overview: Third first-line TB drug; active only in acidic environments (intracellular).
dosage: 20–25 mg/kg OD (max 2 g). Used for first 2 months of standard RHEZ regimen.
mechanism: Prodrug → pyrazinoic acid → disrupts membrane potential and electron transport in acidic pH.
primary_indications: Tuberculosis (first-line — initial 2 months only)
side_effects: Hepatotoxicity; Hyperuricaemia (gout precipitation); Arthralgia; Nausea; Photosensitivity
nursing_considerations: Monitor LFTs and uric acid; Arthralgia: may need allopurinol if gout precipitated; Only used in first 2 months (intensive phase); Sun protection
contraindications: Active hepatic disease, Gout, Severe renal impairment

overview: First-line TB drug with specific risk of optic neuritis — visual monitoring required.
dosage: 15–20 mg/kg OD (max 1.6 g).
mechanism: Inhibits arabinosyl transferase → disrupts arabinogalactan synthesis in mycobacterial cell wall.
primary_indications: Tuberculosis (first-line — intensive phase), Non-tuberculous mycobacterial infections
side_effects: Optic neuritis (visual impairment — RED-GREEN colour blindness first sign); Peripheral neuropathy; Hyperuricaemia; Rash
nursing_considerations: Baseline visual acuity and colour vision test before starting; Monthly visual acuity monitoring; STOP immediately if any visual changes; Avoid if pre-existing eye disease; Renal dose adjustment
contraindications: Optic neuritis, Pre-existing visual impairment, Children <6 years (cannot report visual symptoms)

overview: First-line systemic antifungal for candidiasis; excellent oral bioavailability.
dosage: Vaginal candidiasis: 150 mg PO single dose. Oropharyngeal: 50–100 mg OD × 7–14 days. Systemic: 400 mg loading then 200–400 mg OD.
mechanism: Inhibits ergosterol synthesis (CYP51/14-alpha demethylase) → ↑ membrane permeability.
primary_indications: Vaginal candidiasis (single dose), Oropharyngeal candidiasis, Systemic candidiasis, Cryptococcal meningitis (with amphotericin), Tinea infections (severe)
side_effects: Nausea, GI upset; Hepatotoxicity; QT prolongation; Drug interactions (CYP2C9 inhibitor); Rash; Teratogenic (avoid in pregnancy)
nursing_considerations: Single 150 mg dose effective for uncomplicated vaginal candidiasis; Multiple drug interactions (warfarin, sulfonylureas, phenytoin); Avoid in pregnancy; LFTs if long-term use; Monitor QTc
contraindications: Pregnancy (first trimester), QT prolongation, Hypersensitivity to azoles

overview: Topical/oral antifungal; NOT absorbed systemically. Used for mucosal and skin candidiasis.
dosage: Oral: 100,000 units (1 mL suspension) QDS, swish and swallow. Vaginal: 100,000 unit pessary OD × 14 days.
mechanism: Binds ergosterol in fungal membrane → pores → cell leakage → cell death.
primary_indications: Oral candidiasis (thrush), Vaginal candidiasis (pessary), Intestinal candidiasis, Prophylaxis in immunocompromised
side_effects: Nausea (high oral doses); GI upset; Rash (topical — rare); Bitter taste
nursing_considerations: Oral suspension: swish around mouth and hold before swallowing; Continue for 48 hrs after symptoms resolve; Not absorbed — safe in pregnancy; Dentures: soak in nystatin or treat separately; No systemic drug interactions
contraindications: Hypersensitivity

overview: Gold standard for severe systemic fungal infections; highly nephrotoxic.
dosage: 0.5–1.5 mg/kg IV OD. Test dose (1 mg over 20 min) before first infusion.
mechanism: Binds ergosterol in fungal membrane → irreversible pore formation → cell death.
primary_indications: Invasive aspergillosis, Cryptococcal meningitis, Disseminated candidiasis, Histoplasmosis, Leishmaniasis
side_effects: Nephrotoxicity (major — cumulative); Infusion-related reactions (fever, rigors, hypotension — ; ); Hypokalaemia; Anaemia; Phlebitis
nursing_considerations: Pre-medicate with paracetamol, antihistamine, hydrocortisone (infusion reactions); Hydrate with 1L normal saline before and after (renal protection); Monitor creatinine daily; Monitor K⁺ and Mg²⁺ daily — replace aggressively; Liposomal formulation (AmBisome) less nephrotoxic — preferred where available; SLOW infusion over 4–6 hrs
contraindications: Severe renal impairment (relative), Hypersensitivity

overview: First-line antiviral for herpes simplex virus (HSV) and varicella zoster virus (VZV).
dosage: Genital herpes: 200 mg 5×/day × 5 days. Encephalitis: 10 mg/kg IV TDS × 14–21 days. VZV: 800 mg 5×/day × 7 days.
mechanism: Activated by viral thymidine kinase → inhibits HSV/VZV DNA polymerase → chain termination.
primary_indications: Genital herpes (primary and suppression), Herpes labialis (cold sores), Herpes encephalitis (IV), Varicella (chickenpox), Herpes zoster (shingles)
side_effects: Nausea; Nephrotoxicity (IV — crystalluria); Neurotoxicity (high dose IV — confusion); Phlebitis (IV); Headache
nursing_considerations: IV: adequate hydration to prevent crystalluria in renal tubules; Renal function monitoring with IV use; Start early — most effective within 72 hrs of symptom onset; Topical cream only for labialis — minimal systemic effect; Valaciclovir is better absorbed oral prodrug
contraindications: Hypersensitivity

overview: Oral antiviral for influenza; reduces duration by ~1 day if given within 48 hrs.
dosage: Treatment: 75 mg PO BD × 5 days. Prophylaxis: 75 mg OD × 10 days.
mechanism: Inhibits influenza neuraminidase → prevents viral release from infected cells.
primary_indications: Influenza treatment (within 48 hrs of symptom onset), Influenza prophylaxis (post-exposure), Influenza in high-risk patients (>48 hrs)
side_effects: Nausea, vomiting; Headache; Neuropsychiatric events (rare — in children); Insomnia
nursing_considerations: MUST start within 48 hrs of symptom onset for maximum benefit; Take with food to reduce nausea; Available for NHS prescription — keep stocked in winter; Neuropsychiatric monitoring in children; Renal dose adjustment
contraindications: Hypersensitivity

overview: Most prescribed PPI; irreversibly inhibits gastric acid secretion.
dosage: 20 mg PO OD (standard). 40 mg OD for healing severe oesophagitis or ZE syndrome. H. pylori: 20 mg BD.
mechanism: Irreversibly inhibits H⁺/K⁺-ATPase (proton pump) in gastric parietal cells → ↓ gastric acid.
primary_indications: GERD / reflux oesophagitis, Peptic ulcer disease, H. pylori eradication (triple therapy), NSAID-induced ulcer prevention, Zollinger-Ellison syndrome
side_effects: Headache; GI upset; Hypomagnesaemia (long-term); C. difficile risk; Osteoporosis (long-term); B12 deficiency; Community-acquired pneumonia risk
nursing_considerations: Take 30–60 min before breakfast (proton pump active when you eat); Long-term use: monitor Mg²⁺ and B12 annually; Not for symptom relief on-demand — take regularly; Review need at 4–8 weeks; CYP2C19 metabolism — interactions with clopidogrel
contraindications: Hypersensitivity, Concurrent atazanavir/nelfinavir (reduces ARV absorption)

overview: PPI with fewer drug interactions than omeprazole; available as IV formulation.
dosage: 40 mg PO OD. IV: 40–80 mg OD (ICU). H. pylori: 40 mg BD.
mechanism: Irreversible H⁺/K⁺-ATPase inhibition.
primary_indications: GERD, Peptic ulcer, GI stress ulcer prophylaxis (ICU), H. pylori eradication
side_effects: Headache; GI upset; Hypomagnesaemia; Bone fracture risk (long-term)
nursing_considerations: Preferred PPI with clopidogrel (fewer CYP2C19 interactions); IV formulation: infuse over 15 min; Long-term: electrolyte monitoring; Use lowest effective dose for shortest time
contraindications: PPI hypersensitivity

overview: Antiemetic and prokinetic agent; risk of tardive dyskinesia with prolonged use.
dosage: 10 mg PO/IV/IM TDS. Max 30 mg/day. Max 5 days (acute).
mechanism: D2 antagonist (antiemetic at CTZ) + 5-HT4 agonist (prokinetic).
primary_indications: Nausea and vomiting (post-operative, chemotherapy, migraine), Gastroparesis, Gastro-oesophageal reflux, Hiccups
side_effects: Extrapyramidal reactions (acute dystonia, especially in young women and children); Tardive dyskinesia (long-term); Sedation; Hyperprolactinaemia; Restlessness (akathisia)
nursing_considerations: MAX 5 days use (tardive dyskinesia risk); Acute dystonia: procyclidine or benztropine reversal; Avoid in epilepsy (lowers seizure threshold); Avoid in children <1 year; Monitor for restlessness and involuntary movements
contraindications: GI obstruction, Phaeochromocytoma, Epilepsy, Parkinson disease

overview: Highly effective antiemetic for chemotherapy-induced and post-operative nausea; minimal sedation.
dosage: PONV: 4 mg IV at induction. CINV: 8 mg IV before chemo, then 8 mg PO BD × 5 days.
mechanism: 5-HT3 receptor antagonism in vagal afferents and CTZ.
primary_indications: Chemotherapy-induced nausea and vomiting (CINV), Post-operative nausea and vomiting (PONV), Radiotherapy-induced nausea, Hyperemesis gravidarum (off-label)
side_effects: Headache; Constipation; QT prolongation (dose-dependent); Serotonin syndrome (high doses with serotonergic drugs); Flushing
nursing_considerations: Avoid high IV doses (8 mg in patients with QT risk); ECG monitoring if IV use with cardiac risk; IV: give over 30 seconds to 5 min; Effective without causing sedation; Dissolving tablet (Zofran ODT) for paediatric and nauseated patients
contraindications: Congenital QT prolongation, QT-prolonging drugs

overview: Peripheral D2 antagonist; causes less EPS than metoclopramide (doesn\'t cross BBB); cardiac risk.
dosage: 10 mg PO TDS, 30 min before meals. Max 30 mg/day. Short-term only.
mechanism: Peripheral D2 blockade → prokinetic and antiemetic effect without CNS penetration.
primary_indications: Nausea and vomiting, Gastroparesis, Promotility, Lactation stimulation (off-label)
side_effects: QT prolongation and cardiac arrhythmias (serious); Hyperprolactinaemia (galactorrhoea); Dry mouth; Headache
nursing_considerations: Maximum 7 days use in most patients due to cardiac risk; Contraindicated if QTc >450 ms; Avoid other QT-prolonging drugs; Avoid in hepatic impairment; Less EPS than metoclopramide
contraindications: QT prolongation, Cardiac arrhythmia, Hepatic impairment, Concomitant ketoconazole

overview: Peripheral opioid agonist that reduces intestinal motility; does not cross BBB.
dosage: 4 mg initially, then 2 mg after each loose stool. Max 16 mg/day.
mechanism: Peripheral mu-opioid agonism → ↓ intestinal motility and secretion.
primary_indications: Acute diarrhoea, Chronic diarrhoea (IBD, ileostomy), Travellers\
side_effects: Constipation; Abdominal cramps; Nausea; QT prolongation (high doses); Paralytic ileus
nursing_considerations: Stop if no improvement after 48 hrs; Maintain adequate hydration in acute diarrhoea; ORS (oral rehydration salts) is primary treatment — loperamide adjunct only; Avoid in dysentery (bloody diarrhoea — infectious); Do not use if bowel obstruction suspected
contraindications: Acute dysentery (bloody diarrhoea), Antibiotic-associated pseudomembranous colitis, Bowel obstruction, Children <2 years

overview: Synthetic thyroxine replacement for hypothyroidism; narrow therapeutic index.
dosage: Adults: 50–100 mcg OD; titrate by 25 mcg every 4–6 weeks. Elderly: start 25 mcg.
mechanism: Replaces endogenous T4 → converted to T3 peripherally → binds nuclear receptors → gene transcription.
primary_indications: Primary hypothyroidism, Post-thyroidectomy replacement, Thyroid cancer suppression, Congenital hypothyroidism
side_effects: Symptoms of thyrotoxicosis if overdosed: tachycardia, palpitations, tremor, weight loss, insomnia; Angina/MI (precipitated by over-replacement); Osteoporosis (long-term over-treatment)
nursing_considerations: Take on empty stomach, 30–60 min before food (absorption); Consistent timing daily; Calcium, iron, antacids reduce absorption (take 4 hrs apart); TSH monitoring every 4–6 weeks until stable, then annually; Caution in ischaemic heart disease — start low; Pregnancy: may need dose increase (every trimester)
contraindications: Untreated adrenal insufficiency (adrenal crisis risk), Thyrotoxicosis

overview: First-line treatment for hyperthyroidism in Graves disease; agranulocytosis risk.
dosage: 15–40 mg OD (initial); reduce to 5–15 mg maintenance once euthyroid.
mechanism: Inhibits thyroid peroxidase → ↓ thyroid hormone synthesis.
primary_indications: Hyperthyroidism (Graves disease), Toxic multinodular goitre, Pre-operative preparation for thyroidectomy
side_effects: Agranulocytosis (rare but SERIOUS — 0.1%); Rash, urticaria; Arthralgia; GI upset; Hypothyroidism (over-treatment)
nursing_considerations: Sore throat = STOP and urgent FBC (agranulocytosis — life-threatening); Issue patient warning card; TFTs every 4–6 weeks; Avoid in 1st trimester pregnancy (propylthiouracil preferred); Crosses placenta — fetal hypothyroidism risk; Monitor for signs of hypothyroidism (over-treatment)
contraindications: Agranulocytosis on thionamides, Severe hepatic impairment

overview: Natural cortisol analogue; used for acute adrenal crisis, allergic reactions, and replacement therapy.
dosage: Adrenal crisis: 100 mg IV stat, then 50–100 mg every 6–8 hrs. Replacement: 10–30 mg/day PO in divided doses.
mechanism: Binds glucocorticoid and mineralocorticoid receptors → anti-inflammatory + sodium retention.
primary_indications: Adrenal crisis (Addisonian crisis) — IV, Severe allergic reaction/anaphylaxis (secondary), Addison disease (replacement), Inflammatory conditions (topical and systemic), Status asthmaticus (IV)
side_effects: Hyperglycaemia; Hypertension; Fluid retention; Wound healing impairment; Adrenal suppression
nursing_considerations: Adrenal crisis: treat immediately — do not delay; Patients on long-term steroids: carry steroid emergency card; Sick day rules: double dose if febrile illness; Monitor glucose and BP; IV hydrocortisone does not need slow infusion for crisis management
contraindications: Systemic infections without antimicrobial cover, Live vaccines

overview: Potent long-acting glucocorticoid; crosses blood-brain barrier; no mineralocorticoid activity.
dosage: Cerebral oedema: 4–8 mg IV QDS. Meningitis: 0.15 mg/kg IV QDS × 4 days (start with antibiotics). PONV: 4–8 mg IV.
mechanism: Potent glucocorticoid receptor agonist; 25× more potent than hydrocortisone; minimal mineralocorticoid.
primary_indications: Cerebral oedema, Bacterial meningitis (adjunct), Croup (laryngotracheobronchitis), Antiemetic (PONV, chemotherapy), Airway oedema (extubation), COVID-19 (severe — RECOVERY trial), Adrenal insufficiency (dexamethasone suppression test)
side_effects: Hyperglycaemia; Adrenal suppression; Psychosis/mood changes; Insomnia; Avascular necrosis (prolonged)
nursing_considerations: Meningitis: GIVE before or with first antibiotic dose (reduces neurological complications); Blood glucose monitoring mandatory; Single dose (PONV) has minimal side effects; Dexamethasone suppression test: give at 11 pm, check cortisol at 8 am; No mineralocorticoid activity — does not replace fludrocortisone in Addison
contraindications: Systemic infection without antimicrobial cover, Live vaccines

overview: Non-sedating antihistamine for allergic conditions.
dosage: 10 mg PO OD.
mechanism: Selective, peripheral H1 receptor antagonism; minimal CNS penetration.
primary_indications: Allergic rhinitis (hay fever), Urticaria, Allergic conjunctivitis, Chronic pruritus
side_effects: Headache; Dry mouth (mild); Minimal sedation; GI upset
nursing_considerations: Non-sedating — safe for daytime use; Can be taken with food; Safe in pregnancy (Category B); Renal dose adjustment; Onset within 1–3 hrs
contraindications: Hypersensitivity

overview: Second-generation antihistamine; slightly more sedating than loratadine.
dosage: 10 mg PO OD. Children 2–6 years: 5 mg OD.
mechanism: Selective H1 antagonism with minimal anticholinergic activity.
primary_indications: Allergic rhinitis, Urticaria, Atopic dermatitis, Allergic conjunctivitis
side_effects: Mild sedation (more than loratadine); Dry mouth; Headache; Fatigue
nursing_considerations: Bedtime dosing if sedation is an issue; Renal dose adjustment; More sedating than loratadine — caution in drivers; Available OTC
contraindications: Severe renal impairment, Hypersensitivity to cetirizine or hydroxyzine

overview: Sedating first-generation antihistamine; used for acute allergic reactions and anaphylaxis.
dosage: 4 mg PO every 4–6 hrs. IV/IM: 10–20 mg slowly.
mechanism: H1 receptor antagonism + anticholinergic + CNS effects.
primary_indications: Anaphylaxis (IM/IV — secondary after adrenaline), Acute urticaria, Hay fever, Pruritus, Transfusion reactions
side_effects: Significant sedation; Anticholinergic: dry mouth, urinary retention, constipation; Blurred vision; Impaired psychomotor performance
nursing_considerations: Sedating — do not drive; Anaphylaxis: give AFTER adrenaline (epinephrine) — not instead of; IV administration: inject over 1 min minimum; Avoid in elderly (falls, confusion); Second-line to non-sedating antihistamines for chronic allergy
contraindications: Closed-angle glaucoma, Urinary retention, MAOIs, Premature infants and neonates

overview: Sedating antihistamine and antiemetic; significant anticholinergic side effects.
dosage: Nausea: 25 mg PO/IM at night. Allergy: 25 mg BD.
mechanism: H1 antagonism + D2 antagonism (antiemetic) + anticholinergic.
primary_indications: Nausea and vomiting (motion sickness, hyperemesis), Allergic conditions, Sedation (pre-operative), Vertigo, Pruritus
side_effects: Profound sedation; Anticholinergic effects; EPS (rare); Respiratory depression in children; QT prolongation
nursing_considerations: CONTRAINDICATED in children <2 years (respiratory depression deaths); IV route: severe tissue necrosis — avoid if possible, use deep IM; Sedation assessment; Avoid in elderly (anticholinergic toxicity)
contraindications: Children <2 years, Coma or CNS depression, Porphyria

overview: Recombinant HBsAg vaccine; part of routine immunisation schedule in most countries.
dosage: 3-dose schedule: 0, 1, and 6 months IM. Accelerated: 0, 1, 2 months (then booster at 12 months).
mechanism: Contains recombinant HBsAg → humoral immune response → anti-HBs antibodies.
primary_indications: Routine childhood immunisation, Healthcare workers, High-risk adults (IVDU, sexual exposure), Post-exposure prophylaxis (with HBIG)
side_effects: Injection site soreness; Mild fever; Headache; Fatigue
nursing_considerations: IM injection: deltoid in adults, anterolateral thigh in infants; Check anti-HBs titre after 3rd dose in healthcare workers (>10 mIU/mL = immune); Store in fridge (2–8°C) — never freeze; Immunocompromised may need higher dose or extra doses; HBIG within 12 hrs of needlestick if unvaccinated
contraindications: Anaphylaxis to previous dose, Yeast hypersensitivity (contains yeast)

overview: Live attenuated vaccine for measles, mumps, and rubella; 2-dose schedule.
dosage: 0.5 mL SC. 2-dose schedule.
mechanism: Live attenuated viruses → cellular and humoral immunity → >95% protection for measles.
primary_indications: Routine childhood immunisation (12–15 months, 4–6 years), Susceptible adults, Post-exposure prophylaxis for measles (within 3 days)
side_effects: Injection site pain; Fever (days 7–12); Rash; Transient thrombocytopaenia (rare); Febrile seizures (rare)
nursing_considerations: Live vaccine — do NOT give to immunocompromised or pregnant women; Delay 3–11 months after blood products/immunoglobulin; Check pregnancy status before giving to women of childbearing age; Advise to avoid pregnancy for 1 month after; Store 2–8°C, protect from light
contraindications: Immunocompromised, Pregnancy, Hypersensitivity to neomycin/gelatin, Active untreated TB

overview: Synthetic oxytocin for labour induction and prevention/treatment of PPH.
dosage: Labour induction: IV infusion starting 0.5–2 mU/min, titrate. PPH prevention: 10 units IM or 5 units slow IV. PPH treatment: 40 units in 500 mL IV infusion.
mechanism: Binds oxytocin receptors on uterine myometrium → uterine contractions.
primary_indications: Induction of labour, Augmentation of labour, Prevention of PPH (3rd stage), Treatment of PPH
side_effects: Uterine hyperstimulation/rupture (overdose); Fetal distress (hyperstimulation); Water intoxication/hyponatraemia (antidiuretic effect); Hypotension (IV bolus); Nausea
nursing_considerations: CONTINUOUS electronic fetal monitoring (CTG) during labour induction; Monitor uterine activity (frequency, duration, strength); STOP immediately if hyperstimulation (>5 contractions in 10 min); Fluid restriction and electrolytes if prolonged use; IV BOLUS contraindicated intrapartum (cardiovascular collapse); Count fluid balance strictly
contraindications: Cephalopelvic disproportion, Fetal malpresentation, Previous uterine surgery (relative), Fetal distress

overview: Gold standard for prevention and treatment of eclampsia; also neuroprotection in preterm birth.
dosage: Eclampsia: 4 g IV loading over 5–15 min, then 1–2 g/hr infusion. Continue 24 hrs post-delivery or last seizure.
mechanism: NMDA receptor antagonism + vasodilation + CNS depression → prevents eclamptic seizures.
primary_indications: Pre-eclampsia (prevention of eclampsia), Eclampsia (treatment of seizures), Neuroprotection in preterm birth <32 weeks, Severe asthma (refractory), Hypomagnesaemia
side_effects: Loss of deep tendon reflexes (early toxicity); Respiratory depression (severe toxicity); Cardiac arrest (very high levels); Flushing, sweating; Hypotension; Neonatal hypotonia
nursing_considerations: CHECK PATELLAR REFLEX before every dose — absent reflex = TOXICITY, STOP; Monitor RR: withhold if <12/min; Antidote: Calcium gluconate 1 g IV — always at bedside; Urine output: <25 mL/hr indicates renal impairment → reduce dose; Serum magnesium levels if concerned (therapeutic 2–3.5 mmol/L); Continuous fetal and maternal monitoring
contraindications: Myasthenia gravis, Renal failure (severe), Heart block

drug_class: Prostaglandin E1 analogue', subclass: 'Uterotonic / Cervical ripening
overview: Synthetic prostaglandin used for cervical ripening, PPH, and medical abortion.
dosage: Induction: 25 mcg PV every 4–6 hrs. PPH: 600–1000 mcg sublingual or PR. Ulcer prophylaxis: 200 mcg QDS PO.
mechanism: Prostaglandin E1 receptor agonism → uterine contractions + cervical softening.
primary_indications: Cervical ripening / induction of labour, PPH treatment (2nd line), Medical abortion (with mifepristone), Prevention of NSAID-induced gastric ulcers
side_effects: Uterine hyperstimulation (fetal distress); Fever and chills (sublingual); Nausea, diarrhoea; Shivering
nursing_considerations: Fetal monitoring during induction; Do not use with oxytocin simultaneously (hyperstimulation); Stable at room temperature (unlike prostaglandins); Sublingual route: fever and chills are expected — reassure; Good option for low-resource settings (heat stable)
contraindications: Previous uterine scar (high risk of rupture), Fetal distress, Hypersensitivity

overview: Most widely used IV induction agent. Ultra-short acting (offset within minutes — hepatic conjugation + redistribution). Produces smooth induction, minimal hangover, antiemetic property. Only anaesthetic used for TIVA (total intravenous anaesthesia). Propofol infusion syndrome: rare but fatal with prolonged high-dose infusion.
dosage: Induction: 1–2.5 mg/kg IV (titrate); elderly: 1–1.5 mg/kg. Maintenance: 4–12 mg/kg/hr. Sedation: 0.3–4 mg/kg/hr. Status epilepticus: 1–2 mg/kg bolus, infusion.
mechanism: Enhances GABA-A activity (possibly different site to benzodiazepines) → CNS depression. Also inhibits NMDA receptors and sodium channels.
primary_indications: Induction and maintenance of general anaesthesia, Procedural sedation (ICU, endoscopy), Total intravenous anaesthesia (TIVA), Refractory status epilepticus (ICU)
side_effects: Pain on injection (common — mitigated by lidocaine pretreatment or large vein); Hypotension (vasodilation + negative inotropy); Apnoea at induction; Bradycardia; Propofol infusion syndrome (PIS): metabolic acidosis, hyperlipidaemia, rhabdomyolysis, cardiac failure — rare but fatal; occurs with >4 mg/kg/hr for >48 hrs; Green urine (benign — propofol chromogen metabolites); Antiemetic effect (useful post-operatively)
nursing_considerations: Contains egg lecithin and soybean oil — CHECK allergy history (avoid in soy/egg allergy); Pain on injection: pretreat vein with lidocaine 20–40 mg IV, or use antecubital fossa (large vein); Strict aseptic technique — lipid emulsion is excellent bacterial growth medium; use each vial within 6 hrs of opening; Propofol infusion syndrome: monitor triglycerides, lactate, and CPK in ICU patients on prolonged infusions (>48 hrs at >4 mg/kg/hr); Monitor BP continuously — hypotension common on induction; have vasopressor ready
contraindications: Allergy to soya or eggs (relative — preparation contains soybean oil and egg lecithin), Patients <3 years (use restricted formulation), High lipid states (severe hypertriglyceridaemia)

drug_class: IV General Anaesthetic / Analgesic', subclass: 'Phencyclidine derivative — Dissociative anaesthetic
overview: Unique anaesthetic — produces "dissociative" anaesthesia (patient appears awake but is unaware). Maintains airway reflexes and spontaneous respiration at subanesthetic doses. Sympathomimetic — increases HR and BP (useful in haemodynamic compromise). Used in emergency medicine, paediatrics, and battlefield trauma. Low-dose infusions for refractory depression (esketamine nasal spray licensed).
dosage: Induction: 1–2 mg/kg IV or 4–6 mg/kg IM. Analgesia (sub-anaesthetic): 0.1–0.5 mg/kg IV. Procedural sedation: 1–2 mg/kg IV over 1–2 min.
mechanism: NMDA receptor antagonism → dissociative state. Also opioid receptor agonism (low affinity). Sympathomimetic: inhibits catecholamine reuptake → ↑ HR and BP.
primary_indications: Procedural sedation (paediatrics, emergency), Induction in haemodynamically compromised patients, RSI for severe asthma (bronchodilator), Analgesia (low-dose IV/IM — multimodal), Treatment-resistant depression (esketamine — Spravato), Battlefield/resource-limited anaesthesia
side_effects: Emergence reactions: hallucinations, vivid dreams, dysphoria, agitation (more common in adults; reduced by benzodiazepine premedication); Increased HR and BP (problematic in hypertensives; beneficial in shock); Hypersalivation (administer with glycopyrrolate); Nausea/vomiting; Laryngospasm (rare but can occur); Increased ICP and intraocular pressure; Cystitis with recreational/chronic use
nursing_considerations: EMERGENCE REACTIONS: prophylactic benzodiazepine (midazolam 2 mg IV) greatly reduces emergence phenomena in adults; Maintain quiet environment during recovery; Glycopyrrolate IV before ketamine reduces excessive secretions (important to prevent aspiration); ICP: traditionally avoided in head injury — evidence now challenges this, but discuss with anaesthetist; AIRWAY REFLEXES INTACT at subanesthetic doses — but do not assume patient is protecting their airway; monitor continuously; Acute asthma: ketamine is the preferred induction agent (bronchodilator via catecholamine release)
contraindications: Hypertensive emergency (unless benefits outweigh risks), Conditions where ICP rise is dangerous (raised ICP — relative), Psychiatric conditions with psychosis (relative), Severe cardiovascular disease

overview: Rapid-onset, ultra-short neuromuscular blockade; used for RSI; causes fasciculations.
dosage: 1–1.5 mg/kg IV. Onset 30–60 sec, duration 8–12 min.
mechanism: Acts like ACh at NMJ nicotinic receptors → sustained depolarisation → flaccid paralysis.
primary_indications: Rapid sequence intubation (RSI), Laryngospasm treatment, Facilitation of intubation in emergency
side_effects: Malignant hyperthermia (MH) — RARE but LETHAL; Hyperkalaemia (dangerous in burns, crush injury, denervation); Myalgia (post-fasciculation); Bradycardia (2nd dose); Raised IOP and ICP; Prolonged block in pseudocholinesterase deficiency
nursing_considerations: DANTROLENE must be available when using suxamethonium (MH antidote); MH signs: sustained muscular rigidity + hyperthermia + hypercapnia; CONTRAINDICATED in burns, denervation, myopathy, hyperkalaemia (fatal hyperkalaemia); Check family history of MH or anaesthetic death; Can only be reversed by letting it metabolise (pseudocholinesterase)
contraindications: Hyperkalaemia, Burns (after 24 hrs), Crush injuries, Spinal cord injury (denervation), Personal/family history of malignant hyperthermia, Pseudocholinesterase deficiency, Myopathies

drug_class: Anticholinesterase / NMB Reversal', subclass: 'Quaternary ammonium carbamate
overview: Acetylcholinesterase inhibitor used to reverse non-depolarising NMB. MUST be given with anticholinergic (glycopyrrolate or atropine) to counteract muscarinic side effects (bradycardia, increased secretions). Cannot reverse deep block — only effective when spontaneous recovery has begun (T2 of TOF visible).
dosage: NMB reversal: 0.05 mg/kg (max 5 mg) IV with glycopyrrolate 0.2 mg per 1 mg neostigmine.
mechanism: Reversible inhibition of acetylcholinesterase → acetylcholine accumulates at NMJ → competes with and displaces residual NMB. Also acts at muscarinic receptors (glycopyrrolate counteracts this).
primary_indications: Reversal of non-depolarising NMB (when TOF shows T2), Myasthenia gravis treatment (neostigmine), Paralytic ileus (neostigmine IV)
side_effects: Bradycardia (counteracted by glycopyrrolate); Increased secretions, bronchospasm (muscarinic); GI: nausea, vomiting, increased motility; Cannot reverse deep block (inadequate in high residual NMB — use sugammadex instead)
nursing_considerations: ALWAYS give with glycopyrrolate (preferred — no CNS effect) or atropine — without anticholinergic, severe bradycardia and hypersalivation will occur; TOF monitoring: administer only when T2 is visible (spontaneous recovery begun); neostigmine cannot reverse deep block — causes paradoxical weakness; Monitor HR and BP after administration; Maximum dose: 5 mg — further neostigmine paradoxically causes muscle weakness (depolarising block)
contraindications: Deep neuromuscular block (use sugammadex instead), Intestinal or urinary obstruction, Peritonitis

overview: Mildest topical corticosteroid; OTC available; safe for face and flexures.
dosage: 0.5–2.5% cream/ointment applied BD. Fingertip unit (FTU) system.
mechanism: Local glucocorticoid receptor activation → ↓ inflammatory cytokines → anti-inflammatory.
primary_indications: Mild eczema/atopic dermatitis, Mild inflammatory skin conditions, Insect bite reactions, Intertrigo, Mild psoriasis (face, flexures)
side_effects: Skin thinning (atrophy) — with prolonged use; Striae; Hypopigmentation; Perioral dermatitis (face); Telangiectasia
nursing_considerations: Use Fingertip Unit (FTU) for dosing accuracy; Face and flexures: 7-day max, then review; Apply thinly; Ointment for dry/scaly; cream for moist areas; Not for infected skin without antimicrobial cover
contraindications: Infected skin (without antimicrobial), Rosacea, Acne

overview: Potent corticosteroid used topically for skin disease and systemically/IM for antenatal lung maturity.
dosage: Topical: 0.1% cream/ointment BD. Antenatal: 12 mg IM × 2 doses 24 hrs apart.
mechanism: Potent glucocorticoid → anti-inflammatory, ↑ fetal surfactant production (antenatal use).
primary_indications: Topical: moderate-severe eczema, psoriasis, Antenatal: fetal lung maturation (<34 weeks), Systemic: inflammatory conditions, adrenal insufficiency
side_effects: Topical: skin atrophy, striae (potent — avoid face); Antenatal: maternal hyperglycaemia (treat); Fetal: HPA suppression (transient); Systemic: as per prednisolone
nursing_considerations: Antenatal use: monitor maternal glucose (diabetic patients especially); Topical: never use on face for more than 5–7 days; Antenatal course reduces neonatal RDS, IVH, NEC; Can be given in threatened preterm labour
contraindications: Topical: infected skin (without antimicrobial), Active tuberculosis (systemic)

overview: First-line topical treatment for scabies; single application usually sufficient.
dosage: 5% cream: apply from neck to toes, leave 8–12 hrs, then wash off. Repeat after 1 week.
mechanism: Disrupts voltage-gated sodium channels in Sarcoptes scabiei → paralysis and death.
primary_indications: Scabies, Head lice (lower concentration — 1%)
side_effects: Pruritus initially (part of healing); Burning/stinging; Erythema; Rash
nursing_considerations: Treat all household contacts simultaneously (same day); Apply to all skin folds, between fingers/toes, under nails; Wash all bedding and clothing at >60°C; Pruritus may persist for 2–4 weeks after treatment (does not mean treatment failure); Head: apply if crusted scabies
contraindications: Hypersensitivity to permethrin or chrysanthemums

overview: Non-selective beta-blocker eye drop for glaucoma; significant systemic absorption.
dosage: 0.25–0.5% solution, 1 drop OD–BD.
mechanism: Reduces aqueous humour production → lowers intraocular pressure.
primary_indications: Glaucoma (open-angle), Ocular hypertension
side_effects: Systemic beta-blockade (bradycardia, bronchospasm); Stinging on instillation; Reduced tear production; Corneal anaesthesia
nursing_considerations: Nasolacrimal occlusion technique after instillation (minimise systemic absorption); Contraindicated in asthma — systemic absorption causes bronchospasm; Monitor BP and HR; Do not use in cardiac failure or bradycardia
contraindications: Asthma / COPD, Severe bradycardia, Heart block, Uncontrolled heart failure

overview: Short-acting mydriatic used for fundal examination and refraction.
dosage: 0.5–1% solution, 1–2 drops; onset 15–20 min, duration 4–6 hrs.
mechanism: Muscarinic M3 receptor blockade → pupil dilation (mydriasis) + ciliary muscle paralysis (cycloplegia).
primary_indications: Pupil dilation for fundoscopy, Cycloplegia for refraction, Uveitis (prevents synechiae)
side_effects: Photophobia; Blurred near vision (4–6 hrs); Acute angle-closure glaucoma (precipitated); Systemic anticholinergic effects (elderly, children)
nursing_considerations: Warn about blurred vision and photophobia for several hours; Do not drive after instillation until vision fully recovered; Risk of precipitating acute angle-closure glaucoma in narrow-angle patients; Check for allergy to atropine group before use
contraindications: Narrow-angle glaucoma, Hypersensitivity

overview: Opioid antagonist for opioid overdose reversal; SHORT duration — re-dosing often needed.
dosage: Overdose: 0.4–2 mg IV/IM/SC, repeat every 2–3 min as needed. Infusion if long-acting opioid.
mechanism: Competitive μ, κ, δ opioid receptor antagonism → reverses all opioid effects.
primary_indications: Opioid overdose reversal, Opioid-induced respiratory depression, Post-operative opioid reversal
side_effects: Acute opioid withdrawal (in dependent patients); Pulmonary oedema (rare); Arrhythmias; Agitation, sweating
nursing_considerations: SHORT half-life (30–90 min) — patient may re-narcotise after naloxone wears off; For long-acting opioids (methadone): infusion needed; Monitor for 4+ hrs after last dose; Do not leave patient unobserved after administration; Can precipitate severe withdrawal in opioid-dependent patients — titrate carefully; Community naloxone kits: train family/carers
contraindications: Hypersensitivity

overview: Antidote for paracetamol overdose; MUST be given within 8–10 hrs for maximum efficacy.
dosage: IV infusion: 21-hour three-bag regimen. Bag 1: 150 mg/kg in 200 mL over 1 hr. Bag 2: 50 mg/kg in 500 mL over 4 hrs. Bag 3: 100 mg/kg in 1000 mL over 16 hrs.
mechanism: Replenishes glutathione stores → neutralises toxic NAPQI (paracetamol metabolite) → prevents hepatocyte damage.
primary_indications: Paracetamol overdose, Hepatoprotection in paracetamol toxicity
side_effects: Anaphylactoid reaction (especially bag 1 — not true allergy); Nausea, vomiting; Flushing, urticaria; Bronchospasm
nursing_considerations: ANAPHYLACTOID REACTION: slow infusion or stop bag 1, treat with antihistamine, then restart at slower rate; Draw paracetamol level at 4 hrs post-ingestion; Use Rumack-Matthew nomogram to determine if treatment needed; Most effective within 8 hrs — give immediately if in doubt; Monitor LFTs, INR, creatinine, glucose throughout
contraindications: No absolute contraindications in paracetamol overdose

overview: Benzodiazepine reversal agent; SHORT duration — re-sedation common; caution in mixed OD.
dosage: 200 mcg IV over 30 sec; repeat 100 mcg at 1-min intervals. Max 1 mg (usually 300–600 mcg sufficient).
mechanism: Competitive antagonism at benzodiazepine site of GABA-A receptor.
primary_indications: Benzodiazepine overdose reversal, Reversal of procedural sedation
side_effects: Re-sedation (shorter half-life than benzodiazepines); Seizures (in dependent patients or mixed TCA overdose); Nausea; Anxiety
nursing_considerations: Short half-life (30–60 min) — observe 2+ hrs after administration for re-sedation; DO NOT give in chronic benzodiazepine dependence (precipitates seizures); DO NOT give in mixed TCA/BZD overdose (unmasks TCA toxicity → seizures); Monitor level of consciousness after administration; Infusion may be required for long-acting benzodiazepines
contraindications: Chronic benzodiazepine dependence, Epilepsy controlled by benzodiazepines, TCA co-ingestion

overview: Most commonly used IV fluid for resuscitation and maintenance; hyperchloraemic acidosis risk.
dosage: Resuscitation: 250–500 mL bolus, reassess. Maintenance: 1–2 L/day (based on patient needs).
mechanism: Isotonic crystalloid → distributes in extracellular fluid compartment (25% remains intravascular).
primary_indications: IV fluid resuscitation, Hypovolaemia, Sodium/chloride replacement, Drug dilution vehicle, Hyponatraemia with symptoms
side_effects: Hyperchloraemic metabolic acidosis (large volumes); Fluid overload; Peripheral oedema; Pulmonary oedema
nursing_considerations: Only ~25% remains in intravascular space; Monitor for fluid overload (breath sounds, peripheral oedema); Avoid large volumes in renal failure and heart failure; Balanced crystalloids (Hartmann\; ,
contraindications: Hypernatraemia (relative), Hyperchloraemia (large volumes)

overview: IV potassium replacement; HIGH RISK medication requiring strict protocols.
dosage: IV: max 10–20 mEq/hr (40 mEq/hr only in cardiac monitoring unit). PO: 20–40 mEq/day.
mechanism: Replenishes intracellular and extracellular potassium stores.
primary_indications: Hypokalaemia, Potassium replacement during diuretic therapy, DKA management, Cardiac arrhythmia prevention
side_effects: Hyperkalaemia (overdose — CARDIAC ARREST); Phlebitis (peripheral IV); Nausea (PO); Cardiac arrhythmias
nursing_considerations: HIGH ALERT MEDICATION — must be checked by 2 nurses; NEVER give IV push (cardiac arrest); Max peripheral concentration: 40 mEq/L (irritant — use large bore or central line); Continuous cardiac monitoring if replacing >10 mEq/hr; ECG monitoring for changes (peaked T waves = hyperkalaemia); Always monitor K⁺ levels during replacement
contraindications: Hyperkalaemia, Renal failure (inability to excrete), Addison disease (without medical supervision)

overview: First-line oral iron supplement for iron-deficiency anaemia.
dosage: 200 mg PO OD–TDS (65 mg elemental iron per tablet). Pregnancy: 200 mg BD.
mechanism: Provides elemental iron for haemoglobin, myoglobin, and enzyme synthesis.
primary_indications: Iron-deficiency anaemia, Iron supplementation in pregnancy, Prevention of iron deficiency
side_effects: GI: nausea, constipation, diarrhoea, abdominal pain; Black/dark stools (benign); Teeth staining (liquid form); Iron overload (haemochromatosis risk in hereditary cases)
nursing_considerations: Take on empty stomach (best absorption) but food if GI intolerance; Vitamin C improves absorption (take with orange juice); Antacids, tea, coffee, dairy reduce absorption (take 2 hrs apart); Black stools are normal — reassure; Reticulocyte count increases in 1 week; Hb improves in 4 weeks; Continue for 3 months after Hb normalises (to replenish stores)
contraindications: Haemochromatosis, Haemolytic anaemia, Iron overload, Not recommended in malaria endemic areas without physician guidance

overview: Folic acid supplementation essential before and during pregnancy to prevent neural tube defects.
dosage: NTD prevention: 400 mcg OD (standard risk); 5 mg OD (high risk — prior NTD, antiepileptics, obesity). Deficiency: 5 mg OD × 4 months.
mechanism: Essential cofactor for nucleotide synthesis, DNA replication, and cell division.
primary_indications: Prevention of neural tube defects (periconceptional), Folate-deficiency anaemia, Megaloblastic anaemia, Anticonvulsant-related folate deficiency, Methotrexate-induced folate deficiency
side_effects: Generally very well tolerated; Nausea (rare); Can mask B12 deficiency anaemia (neurological damage continues)
nursing_considerations: Start at least 1 month before conception (ideally 3 months); Continue throughout 1st trimester; High-risk: 5 mg dose (previous NTD, antiepileptics, malabsorption); Never give folic acid alone without checking B12 (masks pernicious anaemia)
contraindications: Undiagnosed anaemia (must rule out B12 deficiency first)

drug_class: Haematopoietic Growth Factor', subclass: 'Recombinant human EPO
overview: Stimulates red blood cell production; used in anaemia of chronic kidney disease and chemotherapy.
dosage: CKD: 50–100 units/kg SC/IV 3× weekly; titrate to Hb target 10–12 g/dL.
mechanism: Binds EPO receptors on erythroid progenitors → stimulates RBC maturation.
primary_indications: Anaemia of chronic kidney disease (on dialysis), Chemotherapy-induced anaemia, Pre-operative autologous blood donation, Anaemia in HIV (zidovudine-treated)
side_effects: Hypertension (major — increased blood viscosity); Thrombosis (stroke, DVT); Pure red cell aplasia (antibody-mediated — rare); Headache; Flu-like symptoms
nursing_considerations: Monitor Hb: avoid over-correction >12 g/dL (↑ cardiovascular events); Monitor BP closely — may worsen hypertension; Iron stores must be replete before and during EPO (functional iron deficiency); SC preferred over IV (longer lasting); Store in fridge
contraindications: Uncontrolled hypertension, Pure red cell aplasia from EPO, Hypersensitivity

drug_class: Vasopressor / Catecholamine / Anaphylaxis Treatment', subclass: 'Alpha and Beta agonist
overview: FIRST-LINE treatment for anaphylaxis (IM anterolateral thigh). In CPR: 1 mg IV every 3–5 min. Multiple routes: IM (anaphylaxis), IV infusion (vasopressor), SC (historical), nebulised (croup), topical (ENT — haemostasis).
dosage: Anaphylaxis: 500 mcg (0.5 mg = 0.5 mL of 1:1000) IM into anterolateral thigh. Child: 10 mcg/kg. CPR: 1 mg IV every 3–5 min. Infusion: 0.01–1 mcg/kg/min.
mechanism: Alpha-1: vasoconstriction → ↑ SVR → reverses vasodilation of anaphylaxis. Beta-1: ↑ HR and contractility → ↑ cardiac output. Beta-2: bronchodilation → reverses bronchospasm.
primary_indications: Anaphylaxis (IM — FIRST LINE), Cardiac arrest (CPR — IV), Severe asthma (nebulised — adjunct), Vasopressor in cardiogenic/distributive shock (infusion), Croup (nebulised racemic), Local anaesthetic additive (vasoconstriction)
side_effects: Tachycardia; Hypertension; Arrhythmias (especially IV); Anxiety and pallor; Tremor; Pulmonary oedema (IV — large doses); Tissue necrosis (IV extravasation)
nursing_considerations: ANAPHYLAXIS: IM into ANTEROLATERAL THIGH (outer mid-thigh) — fastest absorption; NOT deltoid; NOT IV unless trained and monitored; give immediately without delay; POSITION: lay patient flat and raise legs if possible (unless respiratory distress — then semi-recumbent); EPIPEN: ensure patient and carers can demonstrate correct technique; two EpiPens prescribed (carry 2 at all times); GIVE FIRST, THEN ANTIHISTAMINE/STEROID: antihistamines and steroids are ADJUNCTS — adrenaline is the only life-saving treatment; do not delay adrenaline waiting for antihistamines; REPEAT IM after 5 min if no improvement or worsening; IV ADRENALINE for cardiac arrest: 1 mg every 3–5 min — only in controlled resuscitation setting
contraindications: No absolute contraindications in anaphylaxis or cardiac arrest, Relative (non-emergency use): severe hypertension, coronary artery disease, hyperthyroidism

overview: Anticholinergic used for bradycardia, organophosphate poisoning, and as premed.
dosage: Bradycardia: 0.5–1 mg IV bolus; repeat every 3–5 min; max 3 mg. Organophosphate: 2–4 mg IV, repeat every 10–15 min until secretions dry.
mechanism: Muscarinic receptor antagonism → ↑ HR (vagolytic), ↓ secretions, bronchodilation.
primary_indications: Symptomatic bradycardia, Organophosphate/nerve agent poisoning, Pre-operative secretion reduction, Reversal of NMB (with neostigmine), AV block (temporary)
side_effects: Tachycardia; Dry mouth; Urinary retention; Blurred vision; Confusion (elderly); Hyperthermia; Paradoxical bradycardia (very low doses <0.5 mg)
nursing_considerations: Doses <0.5 mg IV can cause paradoxical bradycardia — use full therapeutic dose; Monitor HR continuously during administration; Organophosphate poisoning: end point is drying of secretions, not tachycardia; Caution in elderly — delirium risk; Have pacing available for refractory bradycardia
contraindications: Narrow-angle glaucoma, Urinary retention, Pyloric stenosis, Tachycardia

drug_class: Antiarrhythmic', subclass: 'Purine nucleoside — AV node blocker
overview: Drug of choice for acute termination of SVT. Ultra-short half-life (10–15 sec) — effect lasts only 20–30 sec. Must be given as RAPID IV bolus followed immediately by large saline flush (otherwise drug degrades before reaching heart). Caffeine and theophylline block adenosine receptors — higher doses needed. AVOID in asthma (bronchoconstriction).
dosage: 6 mg rapid IV bolus (antecubital or central vein preferred) → 20 mL saline flush immediately. If no response in 1–2 min: 12 mg. If still no response: repeat 12 mg once more (max 30 mg total).
mechanism: Activates A1 adenosine receptors in AV node → ↑ K⁺ conductance + ↓ Ca²⁺ current → profound transient AV block (2–4 sec of asystole/AV block) → interrupts re-entry circuit in SVT.
primary_indications: SVT (AVNRT, AVRT) — termination, Diagnostic: differentiation of wide-complex tachycardias (blocks AV node → reveals underlying atrial activity)
side_effects: Transient chest pain/pressure (not ischaemic — receptor-mediated); Flushing (intense, brief); Sense of impending doom (very common, very brief — 15–30 sec); Dyspnoea (brief but often distressing); Transient bradycardia/asystole (therapeutic); Bronchospasm (avoid in asthma)
nursing_considerations: SPEED IS CRITICAL: must be injected as RAPID BOLUS (1–2 sec) — if given slowly, drug is metabolised before reaching AV node; use antecubital fossa or central vein; FLUSH IMMEDIATELY: 20 mL saline rapid flush must follow immediately after adenosine injection; WARN PATIENT BEFOREHAND: tell patient they will feel brief (20–30 sec) intense chest pain, breathlessness, and sense of impending doom — terrifying but completely transient; ECG MONITORING continuous — record on rhythm strip during administration; RESUSCITATION EQUIPMENT at bedside (in case of prolonged pause); CAFFEINE/THEOPHYLLINE: block adenosine receptors — may need higher doses (12 mg first dose); DIPYRIDAMOLE: potentiates adenosine (blocks reuptake) — use quarter of normal dose
contraindications: Asthma or significant reactive airway disease (bronchospasm), WPW with AF/flutter (may enhance accessory pathway conduction → VF), 2nd or 3rd degree AV block (without pacemaker), Sick sinus syndrome, Concurrent dipyridamole (unless reduced dose used)

overview: Most effective antiarrhythmic available but with the most complex toxicity profile. Contains 37% iodine by weight → thyroid dysfunction. Multiple organ toxicity with long-term use.
